parentQuestion: A patient undergoes surgery to remove a tumor that has been secreting excessive growth hormone directly into the bloodstream, causing gigantism. The tumor originated in which type of gland?

options: A chemical messenger that promotes body-wide growth
A protein that breaks down fat
scaffolds.explain: GROWTH HORMONE is a CHEMICAL MESSENGER that promotes BODY-WIDE GROWTH. It affects bones and tissues throughout the entire body — systemic effects.

wrongExplains.0: Digestive enzymes work in the GI tract; GH works body-wide.
wrongExplains.2: GH promotes growth, not just fat breakdown.
wrongExplains.3: Hormones are made by the body; vitamins come from food.

scaffolds.q: How do hormones travel to their target cells?
scaffolds.explain: Hormones travel through the BLOODSTREAM to reach their targets. They're chemical messengers released into blood and carried everywhere in the body.

wrongExplains.0: Nerves transmit electrical signals; blood carries hormones.
wrongExplains.2: Blood is the primary transport; some hormones enter lymph.
wrongExplains.3: Hormones travel distances through blood, not just contact.

options: It secretes hormones directly into the bloodstream
scaffolds.explain: ENDOCRINE glands SECRETE HORMONES directly into the BLOODSTREAM. 'Endo' = inside. They're ductless — hormones go straight into blood for system-wide distribution.

wrongExplains.0: Ducts characterize exocrine glands, not endocrine.
wrongExplains.3: Skin secretions come from exocrine glands.

scaffolds.explain: The PITUITARY GLAND produces growth hormone. It's called the 'master gland' because it makes many hormones, including growth hormone (GH).

wrongExplains.0: Thyroid makes thyroid hormones, not GH.
wrongExplains.2: Adrenal makes cortisol and adrenaline, not GH.
wrongExplains.3: Pancreas makes insulin and glucagon, not GH.

options: Excessive height from too much growth hormone during childhood
scaffolds.explain: GIGANTISM results from TOO MUCH GROWTH HORMONE during CHILDHOOD. Excess GH before growth plates close causes extreme height.

wrongExplains.2: It's a hormonal disorder, not primarily bone disease.
wrongExplains.3: It affects the whole body, not just muscles.

scaffolds.q: What happens when a tumor forms in an endocrine gland?
options: The tumor often produces excess hormones continuously
scaffolds.explain: Endocrine tumors often PRODUCE EXCESS HORMONES continuously. They retain secretory function but lose normal feedback control — leading to overproduction.

wrongExplains.0: Many tumors increase, not stop, hormone production.
wrongExplains.3: It's the quantity, not toxicity, that's the problem.

scaffolds.q: How do exocrine glands differ from endocrine glands?
options: Exocrine have ducts and deliver to surfaces; endocrine secrete into blood
scaffolds.explain: EXOCRINE glands have DUCTS and DELIVER TO SURFACES — not into blood. Sweat and salivary glands are exocrine. They don't cause systemic hormone effects like gigantism.

wrongExplains.0: They have fundamentally different delivery mechanisms.
wrongExplains.2: Endocrine glands make hormones; exocrine don't.
wrongExplains.3: Exocrine glands have ducts; endocrine are ductless.

scaffolds.q: If a tumor secretes hormones into the blood, what type of gland did it originate from?
scaffolds.explain: A tumor secreting HORMONES INTO BLOOD must originate from an ENDOCRINE gland. The fact that growth hormone goes directly into blood tells you it's endocrine tissue.

wrongExplains.0: Exocrine glands secrete to surfaces, not blood.
wrongExplains.2: Sweat glands are exocrine; they don't make hormones.
wrongExplains.3: Salivary glands are exocrine; they make saliva, not hormones.

scaffolds.q: A tumor secreting growth hormone into the bloodstream originated in which type of gland?
scaffolds.explain: The tumor originated in an ENDOCRINE gland — specifically the pituitary. Not exocrine (has ducts), not holocrine/apocrine (secretion modes, not gland types). Endocrine = ductless hormone secretion.

wrongExplains.0: Exocrine glands have ducts and don't secrete hormones.
wrongExplains.2: Holocrine describes a secretion mode, not gland type.
wrongExplains.3: Apocrine describes a secretion mode, not gland type.
